title: Social Vulnerability and Prevalence and Treatment for ...
text:
[Automatically generated] From the JAMA Network, 
this is JAMA Psychiatry Author Interviews.
Conversations with authors exploring 
the latest clinical research, reviews,  
and opinions featured in JAMA Psychiatry.
Welcome to the JAMA Psychiatry 
Author Interview Podcast.
I'm your host and web editor, Dr. John Torous.
Today we're thrilled to be discussing a 
new paper just out in JAMA Psychiatry,  
Social Vulnerability and Prevalence in Treatment 
for Mental Health and Substance Use Disorders.
We're about to be joined by the first and senior 
authors of the paper, Dr. Robert D. Gibbons,  
Ph.D. from the University of Chicago and Dr. 
Ronald Kessler, Ph.D. from Harvard University.
Today, there's increased awareness of how 
social vulnerability impacts mental health.
What is the exact relationship between 
social vulnerability or, say, environmental  
deprivation and outcomes like depression 
and anxiety that our patients experience?
To find out, we'll explore the results of a 
brand new study just out in JAMA Psychiatry.
Thank you both, Dr. Gibbons and 
Dr. Kessler, for joining us.
I want to jump in.
We've all heard of these terms around social 
vulnerability, but what does this actually mean?
Social vulnerability is defined as the negative 
effect on communities due to external stressors.
And it's a nuanced term.
Community is vulnerable if the people living in  
that community are in a situation 
where a mass trauma, for example,  
some natural disaster or public health 
crisis, would have a big effect on them.
And the reason why it's an important 
concept is that it helps us to direct  
resources to specific communities that lack the 
resiliency following an adverse community event.
In your paper, you note, social vulnerability 
has garnered broader interest in public  
health due to its close relationship 
with social determinants of health.
How are social determinants of health related 
the same or different from social vulnerability?
It's a great question and 
again, a very nuanced answer.
Social vulnerability links directly to 
social determinants of health or SDOH.
In terms of social vulnerabilities 
relationship with health outcomes.
Now, social factors are important in 
terms of leading to differential exposure.
Some communities have higher risk of 
exposure to some stressors and reactivity.
A mass trauma would have a bigger 
effect on a more vulnerable community.
Statistically, social determinants of health 
are about both main effects and interactions.
And social vulnerability is 
really just about interactions.
That is the reactivity to stressors.
As a result, social vulnerability 
represents a subset of social  
factors that are the elements of 
social determinants of health.
What do we understand today, before your 
research, we'll talk about the new results  
that you and Dr. Kessler have found, 
but what do we understand about that  
relationship between social vulnerability 
and mental health outcomes before your work?
Much of what we knew came from social surveys,  
where participants were asked 
about their mental health history.
For example, have you ever been 
diagnosed with a mental health disorder,  
or have you ever been treated 
for a mental health disorder?
In general, those studies have 
found relationships between  
social vulnerability and affective disorders.
Our study goes beyond the existing research  
by using mental health and substance use 
disorder diagnoses that are derived from  
gold standard structured clinical 
interviews in a national sample.
And let me pass it to Ron to explain further.
Thanks, Robert.
We showed in this study that social 
factors are associated with aggregate  
differences in prevalence of 
mental illness geographically.
But I think it's important for 
us to be clear that we're kind  
of taking a first step here in 
looking at social vulnerability.
These differences we found 
could be due to selection.
So, for example, we looked at some 
neighborhood and community level factors.
If it turns out that there's a lot of people 
with schizophrenia who live on the Bowery,  
that doesn't mean that living on the Bowery is a  
cause of schizophrenia, it could be the 
other way around, so selection bias.
And the other possibilities, of course, are 
that there's this higher exposure to stressors,  
as opposed to higher reactivity to stressors.
And we just don't know from the data we looked 
at here and the associations we've documented.
But what we do know from our work is that 
the aggregate differences of the sort that  
we documented existed, this opens up further 
research opportunities, looking at decomposing  
that aggregate effect, how much of it is due to 
selection, how much to a differential exposure,  
differential vulnerability, and another 
important thing it's worth highlighting  
that Robert and I have talked about subsequently  
is that this line of analysis allows 
us to look at outliers accurately.
So there are some areas that are 
actually doing much better in terms  
of mental health than are predicted by 
our model, and some doing much worse.
And these are places where doing qualitative 
research to figure out what the heck is going  
on in these places that we could export to other 
places that have seemingly high risk factors  
on a structural level that are not doing as well.
So it gives opportunities for exploration, 
for intervention possibilities.
We know there's been a lot of 
cross-sectional research before  
looking at the relationship between 
social vulnerability and mental health.
But as both of you said, there's 
been some selection bias.
There's things that we can't 
learn from the past research.
You also outlined, Dr. Kessler, how if 
we had better data could be more useful  
for kind of precision allocating 
resources, helping build programs.
How would that better understanding of 
social vulnerability be useful overall?
If we actually knew this answer, 
what would you see happening?
Our study demonstrates associations between social  
vulnerability and the prevalence of mental 
health disorders and substance use disorders.
And our analyses also indicate that increased 
social vulnerability is associated with decreased  
mental health and substance use disorder 
treatment for individuals with specific  
conditions like schizophrenia spectrum disorder, 
opioid use disorder, stimulant use disorder.
What's important for a better 
understanding is that additional  
resources are going to be needed 
in socially vulnerable communities  
to develop the service capacities that 
are needed to ensure equitable treatment  
rates between those communities that 
are vulnerable and not vulnerable.
Our paper leads to that better 
understanding and the potential  
for the direction of those resources.
That's kind of a critical thing.
I think it's also important to highlight 
that by focusing on aspects of community  
differences that are modifiable, our results 
can start creating some scaffolding to help  
design target interventions, give some 
insights into what are the kinds of  
things that governments could do to go in and 
intervene with vulnerable communities that are  
doing worse or rather than better 
in the ways that we've found here.
To dive into your results and what you 
found, the first step is to understand  
the social vulnerability metric or 
SVM, social vulnerability metric.
This is a new metric you use in the paper.
What exactly is it?
The social vulnerability metric is the first 
model-based measure of social vulnerability.
Traditional measures like the social vulnerability 
index from the CDC or the area deprivation index,  
they select a series of putative 
indicators of social vulnerability  
or social determinants of health 
and simply sum the individual item  
scores to derive a total score or do so 
within domains to derive a domain score.
By contrast, the social vulnerability metric 
uses multidimensional item response theory to  
differentially weight the items in terms 
of their ability to differentiate high  
and low levels of social vulnerability and how 
easily satisfaction with the item is achieved,  
kind of the difficulty of the item, if you will.
And these weights increase 
the precision of estimation.
They produce stronger associations 
with relevant health outcomes.
And importantly, the IRT model, 
the item response theory model,  
also identifies items that are inconsistent 
with the underlying latent variable of interest,  
so they can be eliminated from the SVM, which 
also improves the quality of measurement.
We have this new metric, the 
social vulnerability metric, SVM.
You've helped us understand there's, of 
course, a relationship to mental health,  
but we don't have the best data on it.
So to really help us move the field forward, you 
guys use a very interesting and large data set.
Can you tell us about what was the 
data set you used for this work?
This is a study carried out by Research 
Triangle Institute in conjunction with their  
work administering for SAMHSA, the National 
Household Survey on Drug Abuse, which is done  
every year, a very large sample, I think around 
70,000 people, face-to-face household interview.
This was a pilot for a new vision 
of what that survey could be,  
it's called the NISDA survey, 
could be done in the future.
And it was a survey of over 20,000 
households in America, the idea being to,  
as Robert mentioned earlier, to get a 
really diagnostic interview with people.
And the way it was done was by developing 
short screening questions that were  
administered to random samples of people 
in these 20,000 households in the country  
to see in a predictive sense who are 
the people who have a high likelihood  
of having a serious mental illness, 
some likelihood and a low likelihood.
And then we differentially sampled across 
those 20,000 people to get 5,000 who were  
administered a SCID interview, a Structured 
Clinical Interview by telephone by a highly  
trained centralized staff of clinical 
interviewers and then using complex  
weighting procedures to generate estimates, 
small area estimates of the prevalence of  
these clinically significant disorders in 
communities and block groups and counties  
in America so that this multi-level 
model could be imposed on the data.
Now, we have to be cautious about 
the results because, unfortunately,  
the COVID pandemic occurred 
right in the middle of the study.
So the design was interrupted, the plan originally 
was to do all of this stuff face to face.
And it turns out we had to do 
it by telephone and by mail.
And it was a relatively low response rate.
But even with those difficult conditions,  
the main goal of the study was to demonstrate 
a proof of concept that this can be done.
And we showed quite clearly that we 
can do this, we can screen effectively,  
we can come up with good estimates of point 
prevalence of small areas in a multi-stage  
clustered stratified area probability 
sample the US household population pull  
out a truly clinical study as opposed to a 
merely descriptive epidemiological study.
From this very rigorous approach, how many people  
and who was the study population that 
you collected this rigorous data on?
Well, it was adults, so 18 and 
above, people living in households.
It was a multi-stage clustered 
area probability sample,  
so a very rigorous study 
based on all the addresses.
There's a master address file for 
every address in the United States,  
and that file was used to draw 
a sample that was representative  
of all the households in areas that 
are represented in the United States.
Another important piece of the study, 
which I'll throw in a pitch for for RTI,  
although it's not used here, is if you're 
interested in really seriously impaired people,  
even though the great majority of the 
US population lives in households,  
a substantial percentage of seriously 
impaired people do not live in households.
They're homeless, or they're in jail, 
or they're in nursing homes or prisons.
This sample did pilot versions of 
trying to get a total population sample,  
which is to say that it not only went to a 
nationally representative sample of households,  
but it went to prisons and jails, it went to 
nursing homes, and it went to homeless shelters.
And again, we were able to show that it's 
possible to go into a systematic sample  
of those kind of places, enumerate 
people who are there, do screens,  
and administer structured clinical interviews.
So the technology is now there to 
do this stuff on an ongoing basis.
Our hope is that the government follows through,  
uses this pile as the basis for doing 
this in the future so we can get more  
rigorous estimates of these multi-level 
area things than we have right now.
I think at the moment, Robert would agree that the  
results are too thin for us to 
base policy decisions on them.
But what we've done, I think very 
clearly, is show that it's possible  
to use this design going forward to build 
up a stronger sample over the course of  
time so that we can really use these area-level 
vulnerability things for real policy purposes.
What are the main outcomes of interest that  
you are going to be collecting in this 
study that we're going to learn about?
The main outcomes in the study 
were individual-level variables  
for mental health and substance 
use disorder, DSM-5 diagnoses,  
and treatment that was received in the 
past 12 months, which were assessed  
by the SCID remotely via telephone using trained 
and closely supervised clinical interviewers.
And the diagnoses that we assessed 
included schizophrenia spectrum disorder,  
major depressive disorder, bipolar I 
disorder, generalized anxiety disorder,  
obsessive compulsive disorder, post-traumatic 
stress disorder, alcohol use disorder,  
sedative use disorder, cannabis use disorder, 
stimulant use disorder, and opioid use disorder.
And we also analyzed two treatment indicators,  
any mental health treatment and any 
substance use disorder treatment.
That's a lot of outcomes, but also I know 
in the paper you have a lot of covariates  
to assess because you're trying to look 
at social vulnerability and mental health.
There's a lot of things you're 
going to have to try to control for.
So maybe let's review what are the main covariates 
that you assessed and wanted to include.
So our primary independent variable were CIP code  
level estimates of the social 
vulnerability metric, the SVM.
And the SVM is a composite measure 
developed from over 200 social  
determinants of health indicators 
from the 2018 AHRQ SDOH database.
And the variables cover social, economic, 
education, built environment, and health care.
And as a comparator to the SVM, we 
also use the area deprivation index,  
which provides a neighborhood ranking 
based on socioeconomic disadvantage.
It's important to note that 
neither the SVM nor the ADI  
included race or ethnicity in their construction.
In addition, covariates that we adjusted for in 
the analysis included zip code population size,  
which affects the precision of the SVM 
scores, and individual level confounders  
measured in the MDPS, like living alone 
or age and gender, race and ethnicity.
But we also, in a sensitivity analysis, adjusted 
for zip code level composition of Black, Hispanic,  
American Indian, Alaskan Native and Asian 
residents to determine whether or not  
the SVM performance varied by racial 
and ethnic neighborhood composition.
It's sometimes hard to cover so many 
covariates and so many outcomes,  
but I think that's a good summary.
And again, I'll refer people to 
the paper for even more details.
But perhaps now to question 
we've all been waiting for,  
there's a lot of important interesting 
results from this rigorous study.
What are the top three that you 
would want listeners to be aware of?
In contrast to previous studies using 
non-clinical symptom-based survey data,  
we found no association between social 
vulnerability and generalized anxiety  
disorder or major depressive disorder, 
the two main effective disorders.
That was a really important 
and surprising finding.
However, there were very strong associations 
of social vulnerability with the prevalence of  
schizophrenia spectrum disorder, stimulant 
use disorder and opioid use disorder.
And importantly, people with these disorders 
reported corresponding decreases in treatment.
But again, caution is needed because 
of the low response rate and the fact  
that the study was done during the time of COVID.
Are there other important results 
you want listeners to be aware of?
I think it's important to emphasize 
methodological results, too.
I mentioned this briefly before, 
but it's important to realize that  
the complex design of this sort can be 
pulled off, that it is possible to get  
an overall national portrait of clinically 
significant mental disorder at small areas.
And secondly, that we documented these strong 
associations of small area level variables.
Robert mentioned that it's at the zip 
code level, but it's also possible to  
do things at block group levels and census tract 
levels, but in neighborhood you could think of.
To be able to show there's certain neighborhoods 
and certain communities that have higher rates  
of mental illness than others based on structural 
characteristics that are potentially modifiable.
So we can and should build on these results 
to initiate studies of neighborhood and  
community level social determinants of 
health, including vulnerability factors.
And as I mentioned before, the possibility of 
case studies to get insights into potentially  
effective neighborhood and community 
level interventions to figure out why  
is it that some places in this scheme seem to 
be doing a heck of a lot better than others,  
even though structurally they look like 
they should be disadvantaged areas.
Which of these results surprised you the most?
Certainly for me, the absence of an 
association between social vulnerability  
and effective disorders was very surprising, 
particularly given the existing literature.
And we attribute this to an 
improved quality of measurement,  
both in terms of social vulnerability, 
as well as in terms of coming up with  
diagnoses of these disorders based 
on structured clinical interviews  
using DSM-5, as opposed to patient 
self-report of their experiences.
For these results, clearly they 
have implications for policy.
What would you want policymakers to take from  
this work and begin to think 
about or maybe even implement?
As I mentioned before, it's premature 
to implement things based on this study.
This particular study was designed specifically 
to be a pilot, albeit a very large pilot.
We need a replication and an expansion 
at a time when we can implement the full  
initially intended design, which we 
were not able to do because of COVID,  
as I mentioned, that would yield a much 
bigger response rate, higher response rate,  
allow us to confirm the stability of the results 
before making any policy recommendations.
But once that's done, major funding sources that 
could be used to support several expansions in  
high-vulnerability areas, including things like 
state, opioid settlement funds, substance abuse,  
mental health service administration grants, 
Medicaid, 1115 section waivers, and so forth.
There's lots and lots of critical 
policy-relevant community-level social  
determinants vulnerability initiatives that 
can be driven by this stuff going forward.
How would you want clinicians or patients 
reading the paper, listening to the podcast,  
to act on these results or begin 
to think about the influence?
Integrating social vulnerability of health and 
the federal quality improvement programs and  
health care quality initiatives could 
also improve health care providers'  
response to community mental health 
and substance abuse disorder needs.
So that's something that's really critical.
For now, the social determinants 
of health literature, as you know,  
is in a relatively primitive space.
There's a lot of work going on to figure 
out how we could use these kind of things.
There's a couple of papers that are in the works 
right now about reviewing these kind of things.
But we now know for sure that 
there are high-risk neighborhoods.
They can be documented.
There are other outlier neighborhoods that 
should be high-risk, according to our things,  
but are seen to be doing very 
well, and we don't know why.
And that opens up a whole interesting possibility  
for developing interventions that could have 
big mental health implications in the future.
I think that's a nice place to conclude, in 
that there's a lot more we have to learn.
And research like this is helping advance 
us, but I think we're going to see continued,  
excellent research coming out, building 
upon these results that you have.
So I'm John Torous, and I've been speaking 
with Dr. Gibbons and Dr. Kessler about social  
vulnerability and the prevalence and treatment 
for mental health and substance use disorders.
You can find a link to the article 
in the episode description.
To follow this and other JAMA Network 
podcasts, please visit us online at  
jamanetworkaudio.com or search for JAMA 
Network wherever you get your podcasts.
This episode was produced by 
Shelley Steffens at JAMA Network.
Thanks for listening.
